Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 21 to 24 of 2024; Suit No 418 of 2018; Suit No 475 of 2018
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd

Facts.Text: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd is an India-incorporated company in the business of manufacturing and trading in jewellery and precious stones, including diamonds, with a sales office in Mumbai and a jewellery manufacturing factory in Sachin, India.
For approximately 15 years leading up to 2015, Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd supplied diamonds to the business operated by Michael Kriss and David Kriss.

In December 2014, Michael Kriss met Rahul Dholakia in Mumbai to discuss a proposed business collaboration between entities associated with the Kriss family and entities associated with Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd.
On 2015-01-13, Amit Shah sent an email to Michael and David Kriss attaching a document titled "Joint Venutre [sic] Between JDM Group and The Jewelry Co. (SRK Group)".

At the end of January 2015, Michael and David Kriss travelled to Mumbai to meet Govind Dholakia in connection with the proposed collaboration between the Kriss-related entities and the SRK-related entities.

From around 2015-04-01, entities related to Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and entities related to the Kriss family began operating a business collaboration involving the supply of diamonds and jewellery to US-based companies associated with both groups.

Under the collaboration starting in 2015, SRK and its related companies supplied diamonds and various kinds of jewellery to US-based affiliated companies of SRK and JDM, with the goods invoiced to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.

From 2015, the goods supplied by SRK and its related entities for the collaboration were shipped directly to US-based companies affiliated with SRK and JDM, while invoices for those goods were issued to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd in Singapore.
The goods supplied under the collaboration were not physically transported to or from J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd in Singapore, and were held in the United States for sale by US retailers.

Under the collaboration, SRK and The Jewelry Company supplied goods for the account of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, while JDM-related entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc sold those goods on consignment to US retailers such as Macy's, Zales and JCPenney.

Between 2015-09-15 and 2017-04-14, SRK issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 42M Invoices, with total payments by J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd amounting to US$42,994,312.66.

Between 2016-09-02 and 2017-08-02, SRK issued 205 invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 23M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$23,400,456.25.

Between 2016-10-08 and 2017-08-01, The Jewelry Company issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 2.2M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$2,211,077.91.
By 2017-08-31, SRK and its related entities had supplied goods invoiced to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd with total invoice amounts of US$66,394,768.91, comprising the 42M Invoices and the 23M Invoices.
On 2017-08-31, the business collaboration between SRK-related entities and JDM-related entities, conducted through J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, came to an end.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd is contractually obliged to pay Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company for goods supplied under invoices issued between 2015 and 2017, and what amounts are payable after accounting for prior payments.
Party_Details.Rule: In a sale of goods arrangement, where a supplier issues invoices to a named company for goods delivered for that company's account, the company is prima facie obliged to pay the invoiced price or the agreed price for those goods, any contrary agreement on pricing, capital treatment, or settlement mechanisms proven on the evidence.
Party_Details.Application: SRK and The Jewelry Company issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied for its account between 2015 and 2017, and J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd made substantial payments on earlier invoices while later invoices remained unpaid. The collaboration structure used J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd as the invoiced entity for goods shipped to US affiliates, and the goods were recorded in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's internal accounting records as purchases alongside purchases from unrelated suppliers. These features align with a sale of goods arrangement in which J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd bears payment obligations for the invoiced goods, any separate capital or reconciliation arrangements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the parties operated under a non-committal business arrangement or a joint venture agreement and concluded that there was a joint venture arrangement but that the pleaded joint venture agreement was not legally enforceable due to uncertainty and failure to prove material terms, including profit distribution and certain pricing terms. The court nonetheless found that, under the joint venture arrangement, the parties agreed that goods supplied pursuant to the collaboration would be treated as equity contributions valued at cost and that the invoices were not intended to create payment liability at the invoiced amounts. On that basis, the court rejected SRK's and The Jewelry Company's primary contractual claims on the full invoice sums and instead allowed restitutionary claims for reasonable compensation based on cost-plus pricing, while also allowing J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd a counter-restitutionary recovery for overpayments on earlier invoices. The court further held that J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's defences of estoppel and change of position did not bar the restitutionary claims and that the alleged joint venture agreement could not ground claims for breach or inducing breach. On appeal, the Appellate Division reconsidered the characterisation of the invoices and the effect of the joint venture arrangement on J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's payment obligations, focusing on whether the goods were supplied as sales and whether the capital characterisation displaced prima facie contractual liability.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 21 to 24 of 2024; Suit No 418 of 2018; Suit No 475 of 2018
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd is a Singapore-incorporated company in the business of trading jewellery and precious stones, incorporated on 2015-03-31 with an issued share capital of US$100 comprising 100 ordinary shares.
On incorporation on 2015-03-31, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd issued 50 shares to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt and 25 shares each to Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss.

On 2015-03-31, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd appointed Ng Chee Wooi Michael as a nominee resident director under a Resident Director Indemnity Agreement, under which he was to act on the instructions of Jim Goldsborough as the authorised person designated by the shareholders.
On 2015-04-15, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss were appointed as directors of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.

From around 2015-04-01, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was used as a vehicle for a business collaboration between entities associated with Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and entities associated with the Kriss family.

In May 2015, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd opened a bank account with Israel Discount Bank in New York, with signatories including Amit Shah, Ashish Shah, Jim Goldsborough, Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss, any one of whom could authorise payments.

From 2015, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was the named invoice recipient for goods supplied by SRK and The Jewelry Company to US-based affiliates, while the goods were physically located in the United States.

Under the collaboration structure from 2015, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was invoiced by SRK and The Jewelry Company for goods that were then sold by JDM-related entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc to US retailers on a consignment basis.
On 2016-05-11, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$500,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who on the same day transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.
On 2016-05-13, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt transferred US$300,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.

On 2016-05-19, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$200,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who on the same day transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, resulting in a total injection of US$700,000 into J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd in May 2016 described in remittance forms as "Capital Introduction".

In September 2016, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd engaged KPMG, an Indian partnership, to carry out an accounting exercise relating to the collaboration, and KPMG withdrew from the engagement in October 2017 citing difficulties in obtaining required support and data.
Between 2015-09-15 and 2017-04-14, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd made payments totalling US$42,994,312.66 to Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd in respect of invoices later described as the 42M Invoices.

In May 2017, daily trial balances of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for 2016 and for January to August 2017 were sent by email to its auditors, showing purchase accounts and payables for goods supplied by SRK, The Jewelry Company and other suppliers.

In 2017, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd requested a comfort letter from SRK to address concerns about its ability to meet payment obligations to SRK and The Jewelry Company for goods supplied, in connection with audit considerations on going concern.
On 2017-08-31, the collaboration under which J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was invoiced for goods supplied by SRK and The Jewelry Company was terminated.
On 2017-12-28, TJC Jewelry, Inc commenced proceedings in New York against JDM-related entities concerning diamonds and jewellery inventory stored in the JDM entities' vault in New York.

On 2018-03-27, JDM-related entities and J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd commenced proceedings in New York against SRK-related entities and Ashish Shah, including claims relating to capital contributions and profits from the collaboration.

On 2018-01-12, Michael Kriss sent J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's auditors a copy of revised financial statements for 2016 and explained that the initial financial statements did not correctly reflect the company's financial position.

On 2018-01-22, Michael and David Kriss and Ng Chee Wooi Michael signed a directors' resolution of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd authorising them to commence and manage legal proceedings against TJC Jewelry, Inc on behalf of the company to recover an amount of US$3,733,503.43 described as owing by TJC Jewelry, Inc.

On 2018-02-15, Michael and David Kriss and Ng Chee Wooi Michael signed a directors' resolution of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd authorising the Kriss brothers to commence and manage legal proceedings against Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company to recover charges they considered to be the responsibility of SRK or its affiliates.

On 2018-03-06, Michael and David Kriss and Ng Chee Wooi Michael signed a directors' resolution of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd approving revised financial statements for 2016 and resolving that the second annual general meeting be convened.
On 2018-04-04, notice was given that an annual general meeting of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd would be held on 2018-04-19 to adopt the revised financial statements for 2016.

On 2018-04-19, an annual general meeting of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was held at which the revised financial statements for 2016 were adopted; Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt did not attend in person or by proxy.

On 2018-04-30, a further directors' resolution was circulated within J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd seeking to appoint solicitors and authorise Michael and David Kriss to act on behalf of the company in relation to Suit 418.

On 2018-03-11, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's US counsel Moses & Singer LLP sent a letter responding to a 2018-03-01 demand from SRK, stating that any amounts due from J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd to SRK were offset and exceeded by amounts due from SRK to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd or the joint venture.

Between 2016-09-02 and 2017-08-02, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd received 205 invoices from Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd for goods supplied, later described as the 23M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$23,400,456.25, which remained unpaid after August 2017.

Between 2016-10-08 and 2017-08-01, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd received invoices from The Jewelry Company for goods supplied, later described as the 2.2M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$2,211,077.91, which remained unpaid after August 2017.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd incurred payment obligations for goods invoiced by Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company between 2015 and 2017, and whether any such obligations are affected by capital treatment, reconciliation mechanisms, or set-off arrangements within the parties' collaboration structure.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company is consistently invoiced for goods supplied for its account and records those goods as purchases in its accounting records, the company is generally treated as the buyer and is obliged to pay the agreed or reasonable price, unless a separate binding arrangement clearly displaces payment liability or provides for a different settlement mechanism such as capitalisation or netting after reconciliation.
Party_Details.Application: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was the named recipient on invoices issued by SRK and The Jewelry Company for goods supplied under the collaboration, and it made substantial payments on earlier invoices while later invoices remained unpaid. Its internal trial balances recorded the goods from SRK and The Jewelry Company as purchases alongside purchases from unrelated suppliers, and its auditors were provided with these records. J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd sought a comfort letter from SRK in 2017 to address concerns about its ability to meet obligations to SRK and The Jewelry Company, indicating that these balances were treated as payables in its financial reporting. In correspondence in March 2018, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's counsel referred to amounts "due" from J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd to SRK and relied on set-off and reconciliation rather than denying liability. These features support treating J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd as bearing payment obligations for the invoiced goods, any proven agreement on capital treatment or reconciliation that might alter timing or net amounts payable.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered whether J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's role as the invoiced entity under the collaboration meant that it was contractually liable for the full invoice amounts, or whether the joint venture arrangement and alleged capital terms meant that the invoices did not create payment obligations. The trial judge accepted that the collaboration operated as a joint venture and that goods were supplied as equity contributions, concluding that the invoices were not intended to attract payment liability at the stated amounts and instead served tax and regulatory purposes. The judge therefore rejected the primary contractual claims and proceeded on a restitutionary basis, using cost-plus pricing as a proxy for reasonable value and allowing J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd a counter-restitutionary recovery for overpayments on earlier invoices. On appeal, the Appellate Division scrutinised the evidence, including J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's trial balances, the comfort letter request, and the Moses & Singer letter, and questioned the coherence of the capital narrative and whether it displaced the prima facie inference from the invoices and accounting records that J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was the buyer with payment obligations. The appellate court also examined J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's alternative defences based on an accounting reconciliation and a termination settlement exercise, and its conspiracy counterclaim, in light of the unenforceability of the pleaded joint venture agreement and the structure of the collaboration.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 475 of 2018; Civil Appeals Nos 23 and 24 of 2024
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt

Facts.Text: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt is an Indian national resident in Hong Kong who worked for Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd from 2005 to 2007 and is a director of S Goldi (Asia) Limited, a Hong Kong-incorporated company that serves as a marketing arm of SRK.
On 2015-03-31, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt was issued 50 shares in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, representing 50% of its share capital.
On 2015-04-15, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt was appointed as a director of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.
On 2016-05-11, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$500,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who then transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd on the same day.
On 2016-05-13, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt transferred US$300,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.
On 2016-05-19, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$200,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who then transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd on the same day.

In early 2018, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt did not sign certain directors' resolutions of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, including resolutions dated 2018-01-22 and 2018-02-15 authorising legal proceedings against TJC Jewelry, Inc, Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company.
On 2018-04-19, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt did not attend the annual general meeting of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd at which the revised financial statements for 2016 were adopted.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether decisions by the Kriss brothers in managing J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, including passing resolutions to commence litigation and making specific adjustments to the company's financial statements, resulted in outcomes that were commercially prejudicial to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt in his capacity as a shareholder.
Party_Details.Rule: In minority shareholder protection, the court assesses whether the conduct of those in control of a company, including the passing of resolutions and financial adjustments, departs from standards of fair dealing and causes prejudice to the shareholder's interests in that capacity, having regard to the company's constitution and any proven understandings among the participants.
Party_Details.Application: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt held 50% of the shares in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd and was a director. After the collaboration ended, the Kriss brothers and the resident director passed resolutions authorising litigation against SRK-related entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc and approving revised financial statements for 2016 that included four significant adjustments. Shailesh did not sign these resolutions and did not attend the meeting adopting the revised financial statements. The adjustments affected reported payables and balances with SRK and the Kriss brothers. The court examined whether these steps, taken without Shailesh's agreement, altered the financial position of the company in a way that impacted his shareholding and whether they were justified by underlying transactions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The trial judge found that Shailesh held his shares in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd as a nominee for SRK but nonetheless had standing to bring an oppression claim. The judge held that some complaints related to Shailesh's position as director or to corporate wrongs and fell outside the scope of minority oppression relief. However, the judge concluded that three of the four adjustments made by the Kriss brothers to the 2016 revised financial statements were not justified and were commercially unfair to Shailesh as a shareholder, and ordered restatement of the 2016, 2017 and 2018 financial statements to remove those adjustments. The judge dismissed J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's counterclaim that Shailesh had breached his duties by not signing resolutions and by requesting information. On appeal, the Appellate Division reviewed the characterisation of Shailesh as a nominee, the scope of his legitimate expectations, and whether the identified adjustments and resolutions constituted commercially unfair conduct toward him in his capacity as shareholder.
